content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers Chapter 7

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 7:1 -- "And it came to pass on the day" vs. "On the day" (va-yehi be-yom)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-it-was on-the-day Moses finished setting-up the-tabernacle" |
| LOGOS | "And it came to pass on the day that Moses finished setting up the tabernacle" |
| PNEUMA | "On the day Moses finished setting up the Tabernacle" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And it came to pass on the day that Moses finished setting up the tabernacle" -- the Hebrew va-yehi is the narrative marker waw-consecutive + hayah ("and it was/came to pass"). EMET preserves the raw morphology; LOGOS renders it with the traditional English equivalent "And it came to pass," which faithfully represents the Hebrew construction. PNEUMA drops va-yehi entirely, losing the conjunction-initial structure required by the binding convention ("And..."). The consensus follows LOGOS, preserving the conjunction and the established English idiom for va-yehi.

---

### 7:1 -- "vessels" vs. "furnishings" vs. "utensils" (kelav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all its vessels" (kelav) |
| LOGOS | "all its furnishings" |
| PNEUMA | "all its furnishings" / "all its utensils" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "all its vessels" -- the Hebrew kelim (plural of keli) is a broad term covering implements, vessels, utensils, and furnishings. In the tabernacle context, kelim designates the sacred implements and containers used in worship. EMET's "vessels" is the more traditional and literal rendering, directly mapping to the Hebrew. LOGOS and PNEUMA use "furnishings," which imports a domestic-furniture connotation not present in the Hebrew. The Leningrad Codex (p. 154) clearly reads כֵּלָיו, and the LXX renders σκεύη ("vessels/utensils"), supporting "vessels." The consensus follows EMET.

---

### 7:2 -- "brought an offering" vs. "made an offering" vs. "came forward with their offerings" (va-yaqrivu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-brought-near the-leaders of Israel" |
| LOGOS | "Then the leaders of Israel... made an offering" |
| PNEUMA | "Then the leaders of Israel... came forward with their offerings" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the leaders of Israel... brought an offering" -- the Hebrew va-yaqrivu is the hiphil of qarav ("to bring near/present"), the standard sacrificial vocabulary for presenting an offering. EMET preserves "brought near"; LOGOS shifts to "made an offering," which is idiomatic but loses the hiphil causative nuance of physical presentation. PNEUMA's "came forward" shifts the focus from the offering to the leaders' movement. The consensus uses "brought an offering," preserving the conjunction "And" (binding convention), the hiphil sense of bringing/presenting, and clear English. The LXX's προσήνεγκαν ("they brought forward") confirms the presentation sense.

---

### 7:2 -- "the ones who stood over those who were numbered" vs. "who had overseen the census" (ha-omedim al ha-pequdim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-ones-standing over the-counted-ones" |
| LOGOS | "they who stood over those who were numbered" |
| PNEUMA | "the tribal chiefs who had overseen the census" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "they were the ones who stood over those who were numbered" -- EMET and LOGOS both preserve the participial construction ha-omedim ("the ones standing") and the passive ha-pequdim ("those who were numbered/counted"). PNEUMA collapses the expression into the interpretive gloss "who had overseen the census," which is accurate in sense but loses the Hebrew's distinctive phrasing. The consensus follows LOGOS's formal rendering, which is both accurate and readable.

---

### 7:3 -- "covered wagons" vs. "covered wagons" (eglot tsav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "six wagons covered" (eglot tsav) |
| LOGOS | "six covered wagons" |
| PNEUMA | "six covered wagons" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "six covered wagons" -- all three translators agree on "covered wagons" for eglot tsav. The rare term tsav is rendered consistently. The LXX's lampenikas ("covered carriages") and the Vulgate's plaustra tecta ("covered wagons") confirm the traditional rendering. The only variation is EMET's word order ("wagons covered" vs. "covered wagons"); the consensus follows normal English adjective placement with LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

### 7:5 -- "each man according to his service" vs. "to each division according to the demands of their work" (ish ke-fi avodato)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "each-man according-to his-service" |
| LOGOS | "each man according to his service" |
| PNEUMA | "to each division according to the demands of their work" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "each man according to his service" -- the Hebrew ish ke-fi avodato is literally "a man according to the mouth of his service." EMET and LOGOS render this identically and accurately. PNEUMA adds "division" (not in the Hebrew) and "demands of" (interpretive expansion), and shifts from singular to plural. The Leningrad Codex reads אִישׁ כְּפִי עֲבֹדָתוֹ clearly on p. 154. The LXX has ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὴν λειτουργίαν αὐτοῦ ("to each according to his ministry"), confirming the singular. EMET/LOGOS are followed.

---

### 7:8 -- "under the hand of" vs. "under the direction of" vs. "served under the direction of" (be-yad)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "by-the-hand of Ithamar" |
| LOGOS | "under the direction of Ithamar" |
| PNEUMA | "All of these served under the direction of Ithamar" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "under the hand of Ithamar" -- the Hebrew be-yad means literally "by/in the hand of." EMET's "by the hand of" is the most literal. LOGOS's "under the direction of" is interpretive but standard. The binding convention favors preserving the Hebrew idiom where it produces intelligible English, and "under the hand of" is both natural and preserves the bodily metaphor that runs through priestly language. PNEUMA adds "All of these served," which is not in the Hebrew. The consensus renders "under the hand of," a slight adjustment of EMET's "by the hand of" for better English idiom while preserving the Hebrew image.

---

### 7:9 -- "upon the shoulder they carried them" vs. "was to be carried on the shoulder" vs. "had to be carried on their own shoulders" (al ha-katef yissa'u)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "upon the-shoulder they-shall-carry" |
| LOGOS | "was to be carried on the shoulder" |
| PNEUMA | "had to be carried on their own shoulders" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "upon the shoulder they carried them" -- the Hebrew al ha-katef yissa'u uses the imperfect yissa'u ("they shall carry/they carried"), preserving the active voice with the Kohathites as subject. LOGOS shifts to passive ("was to be carried"), which reverses the Hebrew grammar. PNEUMA adds "their own," which is interpretive. EMET preserves the Hebrew word order and active voice most faithfully. The consensus follows EMET's structure with minor English smoothing, keeping "upon the shoulder" in its emphatic front position as in the Hebrew.

---

### 7:10 -- "the dedication offering of" vs. "offerings for the dedication of" (khanukat ha-mizbe'akh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-dedication of the-altar" |
| LOGOS | "the dedication offering of the altar" |
| PNEUMA | "offerings for the dedication of the altar" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the dedication offering of the altar" -- the Hebrew khanukat ha-mizbe'akh means literally "the dedication of the altar." LOGOS adds "offering" to clarify that the leaders brought a dedication gift, not the abstract act of dedication. This is a reasonable clarification given that the verb va-yaqrivu ("they brought near") governs the phrase. PNEUMA pluralizes to "offerings," which is not supported by the singular construct. EMET's bare "dedication" is most literal but leaves the syntax incomplete in English. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 7:11 -- "One leader each day, one leader each day" vs. "Each day one leader" (nasi' ekhad la-yom, nasi' ekhad la-yom)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "One leader for-the-day, one leader for-the-day" |
| LOGOS | "One leader each day, one leader each day" |
| PNEUMA | "Each day one leader is to present his offering" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "One leader each day, one leader each day" -- the Hebrew repeats the phrase nasi' ekhad la-yom twice, a deliberate rhetorical doubling visible in the Leningrad Codex. EMET and LOGOS preserve the doubled phrase. PNEUMA reduces it to a single statement and adds "is to present his offering," which is interpretive. The repetition is textually secure and theologically significant -- it emphasizes the deliberate pacing of the dedication. The consensus follows LOGOS, preserving the doubled phrase.

---

### 7:13 -- "dish" vs. "dish" vs. "dish" (qe'arah) / "basin" vs. "basin" vs. "basin" (mizraq)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "one dish of-silver... one bowl of-silver" |
| LOGOS | "one silver dish... one silver basin" |
| PNEUMA | "one silver dish... one silver basin" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "one silver dish... one silver basin" -- for qe'arah, all three use "dish." For mizraq, EMET uses "bowl" while LOGOS and PNEUMA use "basin." The Hebrew mizraq derives from zaraq ("to toss/sprinkle"), referring to a vessel used for collecting and sprinkling sacrificial blood -- a basin rather than a drinking bowl. The LXX renders phialen ("bowl/basin"). "Basin" better captures the cultic function. The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "basin."

---

### 7:14 -- "spoon" vs. "dish" vs. "pan" (kaf)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "One spoon of-ten gold" |
| LOGOS | "one golden dish of ten shekels" |
| PNEUMA | "one gold pan weighing ten shekels" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "one golden spoon of ten shekels" -- the Hebrew kaf literally means "palm/hand" and designates a palm-shaped ladle or spoon used for incense. EMET renders "spoon," which is the traditional and most widely attested English equivalent (cf. KJV, NASB). LOGOS uses "dish," which is the same English word used for the qe'arah in v. 13, creating confusion between two distinct Hebrew terms. PNEUMA uses "pan," which is a reasonable alternative but less traditional. Using "spoon" for kaf maintains clear differentiation from the qe'arah ("dish") and the mizraq ("basin"), and is confirmed by the LXX's thuisken ("incense vessel/censer"). The consensus follows EMET with "spoon," adding "shekels" (implied in the Hebrew) for clarity as LOGOS and PNEUMA do.

---

### 7:16 -- "sin offering" vs. "sin offering" vs. "purification offering" (khattat)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for-a-sin-offering" |
| LOGOS | "for a sin offering" |
| PNEUMA | "as a purification offering" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "for a sin offering" -- the Hebrew khattat derives from the root khata' ("to miss/sin"). EMET and LOGOS render it as "sin offering," the traditional and most literal equivalent. PNEUMA renders "purification offering," following the interpretive tradition (notably Jacob Milgrom) that emphasizes the offering's effect (purification of the sanctuary) rather than its occasion (sin). While Milgrom's insight is valuable, the traditional rendering preserves the direct etymological link to the Hebrew root. The preposition le- ("for") is rendered "for" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than "as" (PNEUMA), preserving the Hebrew's purposive construction. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### 7:17 -- "the sacrifice of the peace offerings" vs. "the sacrifice of peace offerings" vs. "the peace offering" (zevakh ha-shelamim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for-the-sacrifice of the-peace-offerings" |
| LOGOS | "for the sacrifice of peace offerings" |
| PNEUMA | "for the peace offering" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "for the sacrifice of the peace offerings" -- the Hebrew u-le-zevakh ha-shelamim preserves both nouns: zevakh ("sacrifice/slaughter") and shelamim ("peace offerings"). EMET and LOGOS retain both terms; PNEUMA drops zevakh, reducing the phrase. The definite article ha- before shelamim is present in the Hebrew (visible in the Leningrad Codex), supporting "the peace offerings." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS in preserving the full construct phrase with the article.

---

### 7:42 -- "Deuel" (following Leningrad Codex)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Deuel" (De'u'el) |
| LOGOS | "Deuel" |
| PNEUMA | "Deuel" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Deuel" -- all three translators agree, following the Leningrad Codex reading of דְּעוּאֵל with dalet. The LXX Vaticanus reads Ραγουηλ (Raguel/Reuel) in some passages, reflecting the common dalet/resh scribal confusion. The Leningrad Codex is the primary source text and its reading is followed consistently. No disagreement requires resolution; this note is recorded for textual completeness.

---

### 7:84 -- "dishes" vs. "dishes" vs. "pans" for the gold kappot in the summary
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "spoons of gold -- twelve" |
| LOGOS | "twelve golden dishes" |
| PNEUMA | "twelve gold pans" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "twelve golden spoons" -- the same term kappot (plural of kaf) appears in the summary as in the individual offerings. Consistency requires using the same English term throughout; since the consensus established "spoon" for kaf in the individual offerings (v. 14 and parallels), "spoons" is used in the totals. LOGOS's "dishes" is particularly problematic here as it confuses kappot with qe'arot ("dishes"), which are also being totaled in the same verse.

---

### 7:85 -- sentence structure of the totals
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Thirty and-a-hundred silver, the-one dish; and-seventy the-one bowl" |
| LOGOS | "each silver dish weighing one hundred and thirty shekels, and each basin seventy" |
| PNEUMA | "Each silver dish weighed one hundred and thirty shekels, and each basin seventy" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Each silver dish weighed one hundred and thirty shekels and each basin seventy" -- the Hebrew uses a distinctive accounting style, stating the unit weight before the total. EMET preserves the raw Hebrew syntax ("thirty and a hundred silver, the one dish"), which is faithful but opaque in English. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render intelligibly with "each" and "weighing/weighed." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA's approach for readability while preserving all numerical data.

---

### 7:89 -- "And when Moses went in" vs. "And-when-came Moses" vs. "Whenever Moses entered" (u-ve-vo' Mosheh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-when-came Moses unto the-Tent of Meeting" |
| LOGOS | "And when Moses went into the tent of meeting" |
| PNEUMA | "Whenever Moses entered the Tent of Meeting" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when Moses went into the Tent of Meeting" -- the Hebrew u-ve-vo' is the infinitive construct of bo' ("to come/enter") with waw-conjunction and preposition be-. EMET and LOGOS both render with "And when," preserving the conjunction. PNEUMA uses "Whenever," which implies a habitual/iterative action. The hitpa'el participle middabber later in the verse may support an iterative reading, but the infinitive construct u-ve-vo' itself does not specify frequency. The more conservative "And when" preserves the ambiguity of the Hebrew. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 7:89 -- "the Voice" vs. "the voice" -- capitalization
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-voice speaking" |
| LOGOS | "the voice speaking" |
| PNEUMA | "the Voice speaking" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Voice speaking" -- the Hebrew ha-qol ("the voice") here refers to the divine voice emanating from between the cherubim. PNEUMA capitalizes "Voice" to signal the theological weight of this term -- it is not merely any voice but the self-speaking voice of YHWH (the hitpa'el middabber, "speaking of itself"). The LXX Vaticanus adds kuriou ("of the Lord") explicitly, confirming that the early Greek tradition understood this as the divine voice par excellence. Capitalizing "Voice" preserves the theological gravity without adding words not in the Hebrew. The consensus follows PNEUMA.

---

### 7:89 -- "with Him" vs. "with him" vs. "with YHWH" (itto)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to-speak with-him" |
| LOGOS | "to speak with Him" |
| PNEUMA | "to speak with YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to speak with Him" -- the Hebrew itto means "with him," and the referent is YHWH. LOGOS capitalizes "Him" as a reverent pronoun for the divine referent, which the context unambiguously identifies. PNEUMA substitutes "YHWH" for the pronoun, which adds specificity not present in the Hebrew text at this point. EMET uses lowercase "him," which is grammatically correct but misses the opportunity to signal the divine referent. The consensus follows LOGOS with capitalized "Him," a convention already established in previous chapters of this translation.

---

### 7:89 -- "Ark of the Testimony" vs. "ark of the testimony" -- capitalization
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-ark of the-testimony" |
| LOGOS | "the ark of the testimony" |
| PNEUMA | "the Ark of the Testimony" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Ark of the Testimony" -- PNEUMA capitalizes "Ark" and "Testimony" to mark these as sacred technical terms for specific holy objects. The Ark (aron) is a unique cult object, not a generic container, and the Testimony (edut) refers specifically to the covenant tablets. Capitalizing sacred objects and their designations is consistent with the treatment of "Tent of Meeting" (capitalized by all three translators) and is a convention established in prior consensus chapters. The consensus follows PNEUMA.

---

### 7:89 -- "and He spoke to him" vs. "and-he-spoke unto-him" (va-yedabber elav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-he-spoke unto-him" |
| LOGOS | "and He spoke to him" |
| PNEUMA | "And the Voice spoke to him" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and He spoke to him" -- the Hebrew va-yedabber elav is simply "and he spoke to him." The subject is YHWH, so LOGOS's capitalized "He" correctly identifies the divine subject. PNEUMA substitutes "the Voice" as subject, which is interpretive -- the Hebrew uses a standard third-person verb form, not the noun qol ("voice") again. EMET's lowercase "he" is ambiguous. The consensus follows LOGOS, capitalizing "He" for the divine referent while preserving the brevity of the Hebrew clause.

---

### General note -- Structural repetition (vv. 12–83)
| Translator | Approach |
|---|---|
| EMET | Full repetition with extensive bracketed transliterations and notes for each iteration |
| LOGOS | Full repetition in clean prose, notes deferred to end-of-chapter section |
| PNEUMA | Full repetition in clean prose, notes deferred to end-of-chapter section |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three translators rightly preserve the full twelve-fold repetition without abbreviation, honoring the deliberate literary and theological structure of the Hebrew text. The Leningrad Codex (pp. 154-157) and the Vaticanus LXX (pa_0116-0118) both preserve every iteration in full, confirming the antiquity and intentionality of this structure. The consensus follows LOGOS's clean prose style for the repeated sections, allowing the formulaic dignity of the text to speak for itself without inline scholarly apparatus. The offering sequence follows the camp/march order of Numbers 2: East (Judah, Issachar, Zebulun -- days 1-3), South (Reuben, Simeon, Gad -- days 4-6), West (Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin -- days 7-9), North (Dan, Asher, Naphtali -- days 10-12).
